seo.title: E-Commerce Research Agents AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for research agents AI agents in e-commerce. Covers FTC advertising guidelines, CCPA/GDPR, PCI DSS for payments.

content.intro: E-Commerce research agents that autonomously gather and synthesize product, pricing, and policy information carry significant compliance risk — a single inaccurate claim or outdated price can trigger FTC enforcement, refund disputes, or customer churn. Deploying these agents responsibly requires rigorous validation pipelines, source credibility checks, and real-time data freshness controls. This checklist provides concrete, actionable steps to ensure your research agents operate within FTC, CCPA/GDPR, PCI DSS, and ADA requirements while protecting brand reputation and revenue.

sections.heading: Source Verification & Data Integrity
sections.description: Research agents must only synthesize information from verified, authoritative sources. Unverified or stale data fed into product listings, pricing displays, or policy summaries can directly violate FTC substantiation requirements and mislead consumers.

items.label: Maintain an approved source allowlist
items.description: Configure the agent to query only pre-approved domains (e.g., manufacturer sites, official retailer APIs, regulatory databases). Block scraping from user-generated content sites, forums, or unverified third-party aggregators for any claim that will be surfaced to customers.

items.label: Implement source credibility scoring
items.description: Assign a credibility score (1–5) to each source based on domain authority, publication recency, and editorial standards. Require a minimum score threshold before any sourced claim is included in agent output used for product descriptions or pricing.

items.label: Cross-reference product claims across at least two independent sources
items.description: For any health, performance, or comparative product claim, require the agent to find corroboration from at least two independent, authoritative sources before surfacing the claim. Log both sources with timestamps in the audit trail.

items.label: Validate URLs and canonical source identity before ingestion
items.description: Run automated checks to confirm that scraped URLs resolve to the intended domain (not redirects to third parties) and that SSL certificates are valid. Flag and quarantine content from domains with recent WHOIS changes.

items.label: Detect and flag AI-generated or synthetic source content
items.description: Integrate an AI-content detection layer to identify if a source page was itself generated by an LLM. Treat such sources as unverified and require human review before any claims are used in customer-facing outputs.

items.label: Log full provenance chain for every synthesized output
items.description: Store a structured provenance record (source URL, retrieval timestamp, page hash, agent step ID) for every piece of information included in agent-generated summaries. This log must be queryable for FTC audit or dispute resolution purposes.

items.label: Reject sources older than a configurable recency threshold
items.description: Set a default maximum source age of 30 days for pricing data and 90 days for product specifications. Allow category-level overrides but require explicit human approval to extend thresholds beyond defaults.

sections.heading: Claim Accuracy & FTC Compliance
sections.description: FTC guidelines require that all product claims — including comparative, performance, and pricing claims — be truthful, non-deceptive, and substantiated before publication. Research agents that auto-populate listings or summaries must enforce these standards programmatically.

items.label: Implement a claim classification pipeline
items.description: Before any agent-generated text is published, run it through a classifier that tags sentences as 'factual claim,' 'comparative claim,' 'superlative claim,' or 'opinion.' Apply stricter substantiation rules to factual and comparative claims, blocking publication until sources are attached.

items.label: Block unsubstantiated superlatives and absolute claims
items.description: Configure a regex and NLP filter to detect phrases like 'best in class,' 'clinically proven,' 'guaranteed to,' or '#1 rated' in agent outputs. Require the agent to either attach a verifiable citation or rewrite the claim before it proceeds to any customer-facing surface.

items.label: Validate pricing data against live inventory system before display
items.description: Before any agent-synthesized price is rendered to a customer, make a synchronous API call to the authoritative pricing/inventory system to confirm the price is current. Never display a cached or researched price without real-time confirmation.

items.label: Enforce accurate representation of return and refund policies
items.description: When the agent summarizes or references return/refund policies, require it to pull the policy text directly from the official policy endpoint (not from third-party summaries). Include a 'last verified' timestamp and a direct link to the full policy in all customer-facing outputs.

items.label: Flag comparative claims that reference competitor pricing
items.description: Automatically route any agent output containing competitor brand names alongside pricing or performance comparisons to a human compliance reviewer before publication. Log the review decision and reviewer ID for audit purposes.

items.label: Implement a confidence threshold gate for synthesized summaries
items.description: Require the agent to output a confidence score for each synthesized claim. Block any claim with confidence below 0.75 from automatic publication and queue it for human review. Surface the confidence score and source list to the reviewer.

items.label: Conduct quarterly adversarial testing of claim accuracy
items.description: Every quarter, run a structured red-team exercise where QA staff submit ambiguous or edge-case product queries to the research agent and evaluate whether outputs contain unsubstantiated claims, outdated prices, or policy misrepresentations. Document findings and remediation steps.

sections.heading: Privacy, Data Handling & Consumer Rights (CCPA/GDPR)
sections.description: Research agents that process customer queries, browsing behavior, or purchase history to personalize results must comply with CCPA and GDPR data minimization, consent, and deletion requirements. Agents must not inadvertently expose PII or retain consumer data beyond authorized purposes.

items.label: Classify all input data by PII sensitivity before agent processing
items.description: Before passing any user-provided input (search queries, account data, browsing history) to the research agent, run it through a PII classifier. Strip or pseudonymize fields classified as sensitive (name, email, purchase history) unless the agent explicitly requires them and consent is documented.

items.label: Enforce data minimization in agent context windows
items.description: Limit the agent's context window to only the data fields necessary for the current research task. Do not pass full customer profiles, payment details, or historical order data to the agent unless the specific task requires it and is covered by the user's consent scope.

items.label: Implement agent session data purge within 24 hours
items.description: Ensure that all intermediate agent state, retrieved web content, and synthesized outputs tied to a specific user session are purged from agent memory and temporary storage within 24 hours of session completion, unless the user has explicitly opted into longer retention.

items.label: Honor CCPA/GDPR deletion requests across agent logs
items.description: Map all agent-generated logs and provenance records to the user ID that triggered them. Ensure your data deletion workflow covers agent logs, not just transactional databases. Test deletion completeness quarterly by attempting to retrieve deleted user records from agent log stores.

items.label: Disclose AI agent involvement in research outputs to consumers
items.description: When research agent outputs are surfaced to customers (e.g., 'AI-generated product summary'), include a visible disclosure label. This satisfies FTC guidance on AI-generated content and builds consumer trust. Do not present agent-synthesized content as human-curated without disclosure.

items.label: Ensure agent interfaces meet ADA WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility standards
items.description: Audit all customer-facing UI components that display agent outputs (product summaries, price comparisons, policy explanations) for WCAG 2.1 AA compliance. This includes screen reader compatibility, sufficient color contrast, and keyboard navigability for all interactive elements.

items.label: Prohibit agent access to payment card data (PCI DSS scope isolation)
items.description: Architect the research agent so it operates entirely outside the PCI DSS cardholder data environment. The agent must never receive, process, or log full card numbers, CVVs, or expiration dates. Enforce this via network segmentation and access control lists, not just policy.

items.label: Maintain a ROPA entry for research agent data processing activities
items.description: Add a dedicated Record of Processing Activities (ROPA) entry for the research agent covering: data categories processed, legal basis, retention periods, third-party data transfers (e.g., external search APIs), and cross-border transfer mechanisms. Review and update this record at each major agent version release.

sections.heading: Bias Detection, Monitoring & Human Oversight
sections.description: Multi-step research agents can introduce systematic bias through source selection, synthesis weighting, or query framing — leading to skewed product recommendations, unfair pricing comparisons, or discriminatory content. Ongoing monitoring and human-in-the-loop controls are essential to catch and correct these issues before they cause customer harm or regulatory exposure.

items.label: Run automated bias audits on agent output distributions monthly
items.description: Each month, sample 500+ agent-generated product summaries and price comparisons. Analyze whether certain brands, price tiers, or demographic-coded product categories are systematically over- or under-represented in outputs. Document findings and adjust source weighting or query templates if bias is detected.

items.label: Implement a human review queue for high-stakes agent outputs
items.description: Define a set of high-stakes output categories (e.g., health product claims, financial product comparisons, any output that will be used in paid advertising). Route 100% of these outputs through a human reviewer before publication. Track review turnaround time and approval rates as operational KPIs.

items.label: Monitor agent query reformulation for prompt injection or manipulation
items.description: Log all intermediate search queries generated by the agent during multi-step research tasks. Run automated checks to detect if external web content has injected instructions into the agent's reasoning chain (prompt injection). Alert the security team immediately if injection patterns are detected.

items.label: Set up real-time alerts for pricing anomalies in agent outputs
items.description: Configure a monitoring rule that fires an alert if any agent-synthesized price deviates by more than 5% from the live inventory system price. Automatically suppress the anomalous output from customer display until the discrepancy is resolved and logged.

items.label: Establish a customer feedback loop for agent output corrections
items.description: Add a 'Report inaccuracy' button to all customer-facing agent-generated content. Route reports to a dedicated queue reviewed within 4 business hours. Track correction rate and mean time to correction as compliance metrics reported to leadership monthly.

items.label: Conduct semi-annual third-party audits of agent decision logic
items.description: Engage an independent third party to audit the research agent's source selection logic, synthesis prompts, and output filtering rules every six months. Require the auditor to produce a written report with findings and remediation recommendations. Store audit reports for a minimum of 3 years.

items.label: Define and test a kill switch for immediate agent shutdown
items.description: Implement a documented, tested procedure to disable the research agent within 15 minutes of a critical compliance incident (e.g., mass pricing error, FTC inquiry, data breach). The kill switch must be accessible to at least three named individuals and tested in a drill at least once per year.

notes.regulation: FTC Advertising Guidelines & Endorsement Guides (16 CFR Part 255)
notes.note: Any product claim surfaced by a research agent that will appear in advertising, product listings, or promotional content must be substantiated by competent and reliable evidence before publication. The FTC's 2023 updated Endorsement Guides also require clear disclosure when AI tools generate or materially contribute to consumer-facing content. Failure to substantiate claims or disclose AI involvement can result in civil penalties up to $50,120 per violation.

notes.regulation: CCPA (Cal. Civ. Code §1798.100 et seq.) & GDPR (EU 2016/679)
notes.note: If your research agent processes personal data of California residents or EU data subjects — including behavioral data used to personalize research outputs — you must provide opt-out rights (CCPA) or a valid legal basis such as legitimate interest or consent (GDPR). Automated decision-making that materially affects consumers (e.g., personalized pricing) may trigger GDPR Article 22 rights to human review. Ensure your agent's data flows are documented in your ROPA and covered by your privacy policy.

Research agents must be explicitly scoped out of the cardholder data environment (CDE). Under PCI DSS v4.0, any system component that could impact the security of payment card data is considered in-scope. If your agent infrastructure shares network segments or authentication systems with payment processing components, it may inadvertently expand your PCI scope and audit burden. Conduct a formal scoping exercise with your QSA before deploying agents in a production e-commerce environment.

Federal courts have consistently held that e-commerce websites are 'places of public accommodation' under ADA Title III, requiring accessible design. All customer-facing interfaces that display research agent outputs — including dynamically generated product summaries, price tables, and policy explanations — must conform to WCAG 2.1 Level AA. Pay particular attention to dynamically injected content (common in agent-driven UIs), which must be announced to screen readers via ARIA live regions and must not rely solely on color to convey meaning.

cta.heading: Automate Your E-Commerce Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your research agents against FTC, CCPA/GDPR, and PCI DSS requirements — catching pricing anomalies, unsubstantiated claims, and data handling violations before they reach your customers.
